---
title: "Geopolitical Uncertainty and SME Capital Investment in Europe"
subtitle: "A Panel Data Analysis"
author: "Bettina Hofmann | ExInt II | WU Vienna | SS 2026"
date: "June 2026"
bibliography: references/library.bib
csl: references/apa.csl
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.3
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---
\begin{center}\small https://github.com/bettinahfmnn/geopolitical-uncertainty-sme-internationalization\end{center}

```{python}
#| label: setup
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

summary  = pd.read_csv("output/tables/summary_statistics.csv", index_col=0)
results  = pd.read_csv("output/tables/regression_results.csv", index_col=0)
panel    = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/panel_with_vars.parquet")

n_obs       = int(panel.shape[0])
n_firms     = int(panel["gvkey"].nunique())
n_countries = 23
capx_median = round(panel["capx_intensity_dv"].median(), 3)
capx_mean   = round(panel["capx_intensity_dv"].mean(), 3)
gpr_mean    = round(panel["gpr"].mean(), 1)
gpr_min     = round(panel["gpr"].min(), 1)
gpr_max     = round(panel["gpr"].max(), 1)

def extract_coef(cell):
    return float(str(cell).replace("***","").replace("**","").replace("*","").strip())

b_gpr    = round(extract_coef(results.loc["gpr", "(2) FE"]), 6)
b_int    = round(extract_coef(results.loc["gpr_x_size", "(3) FE+H2"]), 6)
b_ols    = round(extract_coef(results.loc["gpr", "(1) OLS"]), 6)
bias_pct = round(abs((b_ols - b_gpr) / b_ols) * 100, 0)
```

# Introduction

Geopolitical risk — defined as the threat of adverse events arising from
wars, terrorism, and political tensions between states — has increased
markedly since 2015, with episodes such as Brexit, the US-China trade
conflict, and the Russian invasion of Ukraine creating sustained
uncertainty for firms operating in open economies
[@caldaraMeasuringGeopoliticalRisk2022]. For small and medium-sized
enterprises (SMEs), which lack the diversification, financial buffers,
and political access available to large multinationals, geopolitical
uncertainty poses a particular challenge to capital investment decisions.
Yet the empirical literature has focused predominantly on large firms,
leaving the question of how SMEs adjust their capital expenditure in
response to geopolitical risk largely unanswered.

The theoretical mechanisms linking uncertainty to investment are
well-established. Real options theory predicts that firms facing
irreversible investment decisions will delay capital expenditure when
uncertainty increases, as waiting preserves the option value of
flexibility [@bloomImpactUncertaintyShocks2009]. This wait-and-see
dynamic should be especially pronounced for resource-constrained SMEs,
which face higher relative costs of financial distress and have fewer
alternative funding sources to buffer investment plans against adverse
shocks [@luInternationalizationPerformanceSMEs2001].

This research note examines whether geopolitical uncertainty reduces
capital investment among European SMEs, and whether firm size moderates
this relationship. Using a panel of `{python} f"{n_obs:,}"` firm-years
from `{python} f"{n_firms:,}"` firms across `{python} n_countries`
European countries over fiscal years 2015–2024, we exploit within-firm
variation in capital intensity against annual variation in the
Geopolitical Risk (GPR) index of @caldaraMeasuringGeopoliticalRisk2022.

> **Research question:** Does geopolitical uncertainty reduce capital
> investment among European SMEs, and does firm size moderate this
> relationship?

# Theoretical Background and Hypotheses

## Geopolitical Risk and Capital Investment

The Uppsala model of internationalisation
[@johansonInternationalizationProcessFirm1977] posits that firms expand
their foreign commitment incrementally, calibrating investment to the
perceived uncertainty of the operating environment. Under this framework,
rising geopolitical risk increases perceived environmental uncertainty,
raising the subjective probability of adverse outcomes and reducing the
expected returns to irreversible capital investment. This logic is
reinforced by real options theory [@bloomImpactUncertaintyShocks2009]:
when investment is costly to reverse and the future is uncertain, the
option value of waiting dominates the opportunity cost of inaction,
leading firms to defer capital expenditure.

For SMEs, these mechanisms are amplified by structural vulnerability.
Unlike large multinationals, SMEs typically lack access to deep capital
markets, operate with thin liquidity buffers, and depend on relationship
lending that is itself sensitive to macroeconomic and geopolitical
conditions [@luInternationalizationPerformanceSMEs2001]. The liability
of smallness — the disproportionate exposure of small firms to
environmental shocks — implies that geopolitical uncertainty should
reduce capital investment more sharply for SMEs than for large firms
[@zaheerOVERCOMINGLIABILITYFOREIGNNESS1995]. Empirical evidence from
related uncertainty measures supports this prediction: policy uncertainty
reduces corporate investment, with effects concentrated among
financially constrained firms [@gulenPolicyUncertaintyCorporate2015].

> **H1:** Higher geopolitical risk reduces capital investment intensity
> among European SMEs.
>
> *(Test: $\beta$(GPR) < 0 and statistically significant)*

## The Moderating Role of Firm Size

While geopolitical uncertainty should reduce capital investment broadly,
its effect is unlikely to be homogeneous across the SME size spectrum.
Larger firms — even within the SME category — possess greater resource
slack, more diversified revenue streams, and stronger relationships with
financial intermediaries that allow them to sustain investment plans
during periods of elevated uncertainty
[@johansonInternationalizationProcessFirm1977;
@luInternationalizationPerformanceSMEs2001]. The liability of foreignness
framework [@zaheerOVERCOMINGLIABILITYFOREIGNNESS1995] suggests that
organisational resources and legitimacy — both of which scale with firm
size — reduce exposure to external environmental threats.

Larger SMEs can spread fixed investment costs over a larger asset base,
reducing the per-unit impact of any reduction in capital expenditure.
They also have superior access to external finance, enabling them to
maintain investment through short-term uncertainty spikes rather than
cutting capital budgets. Both channels predict that the negative effect
of geopolitical risk on capital investment should be attenuated for
larger firms.

> **H2:** Firm size positively moderates the relationship between
> geopolitical risk and capital investment — larger SMEs reduce capital
> expenditure less in response to geopolitical uncertainty.
>
> *(Test: $\beta$(GPR $\times$ ln\_at) > 0)*

# Data and Method

## Sample

The analysis draws on annual firm-level data from WRDS Compustat Global
(`comp_global_daily.g_funda`) for 23 European countries over fiscal
years 2015–2024. Firms are classified as SMEs following the EU
definition: fewer than 250 employees or total assets not exceeding
€43 million. Data quality filters require positive total assets
exceeding €100,000, positive sales, positive stockholders' equity,
and total assets of at least €1 million. A minimum of three consecutive
firm-year observations is required for within-firm identification.
The resulting unbalanced panel comprises `{python} f"{n_obs:,}"`
firm-years from `{python} f"{n_firms:,}"` unique firms.

## Variables

**Dependent variable.** Capital intensity is measured as capital
expenditure divided by total assets (`capx / at`). This ratio is the
standard operationalisation of firm-level investment activity in the
corporate finance and international business literatures, since it
scales the absolute euro amount spent on fixed assets by firm size,
allowing comparison across firms of very different sizes within the
same panel. Capital intensity therefore captures the *rate* at which
a firm reinvests in tangible productive capacity each year, rather
than its raw investment volume — which is the appropriate measure for
testing whether firms slow down or accelerate investment in response
to geopolitical uncertainty.

**Independent variable.** Geopolitical risk is measured using the
annual average of the GPR index developed by
@caldaraMeasuringGeopoliticalRisk2022, normalised to a baseline of
100 for 1985–2019. The index ranged from `{python} gpr_min` to
`{python} gpr_max` over our sample period, with a mean of
`{python} gpr_mean`. The GPR index was chosen over alternative
uncertainty measures (such as the VIX or national policy uncertainty
indices) because it is explicitly designed to capture geopolitical —
rather than purely financial or domestic policy — sources of
uncertainty, and because it is constructed from a consistent
news-based methodology spanning our full sample period across all
23 countries in the panel. We use the global index rather than the
country-specific GPRC sub-indices because the country-level series
are not normalised to a common baseline and are therefore not
comparable in levels across countries.
Although GPR is a continuous index in principle, our operationalisation
assigns one annual value per fiscal year, since the underlying GPR
series is published at monthly frequency and averaged to the
fiscal-year level to match the annual frequency of the Compustat
panel. Consequently, GPR takes only as many distinct values as there
are sample years (ten, for 2015–2024), even though the variable itself
is continuous in levels. This explains why Figure 1 shows GPR
observations clustering into vertical bands rather than a smooth
continuous spread: each band corresponds to one fiscal year, populated
by all firms observed in that year. This is a feature of the research
design, not a data error — the identifying variation in GPR is
inherently at the year level, which is also why year fixed effects
cannot be included in the regression (Section 3.3).

**Moderator.** Firm size is measured as the natural logarithm of
total assets (`log(at)`). The logarithmic transformation is standard
practice in the firm size literature because total assets are
strongly right-skewed within the SME population — a small number of
firms near the upper SME threshold (€43 million) would otherwise
dominate the variance of the raw measure. Taking logs compresses this
skew and makes the size effect interpretable as approximately
proportional: a one-unit increase in `ln_at` corresponds to a
constant percentage increase in total assets, regardless of the
firm's starting size. Total assets, rather than employee count, was
selected as the size proxy because it is directly available and
consistently reported in Compustat Global, whereas employee counts
are missing for a substantial share of firm-years.

**Control variables.** We include leverage (`dltt / at`), return on
assets (`nicon / at`), and cash ratio (`che / at`). Leverage controls
for capital structure, since highly leveraged firms face tighter debt
covenants and reduced financial flexibility, which independently
constrains capital expenditure regardless of geopolitical conditions.
Return on assets controls for underlying profitability, since more
profitable firms generate more internal funds available for
reinvestment, which could otherwise confound the GPR-investment
relationship if profitability is correlated with macroeconomic cycles
that also drive GPR. Cash ratio controls for liquidity and financial
slack, since firms holding larger cash buffers are less constrained
in their ability to maintain investment during uncertain periods,
directly addressing the resource-based mechanism underlying H2. All
three controls are standard in the corporate investment literature
and are scaled by total assets for consistency with the dependent
variable. All continuous variables except log assets are winsorized
at the 1st and 99th percentiles [@wilcoxIntroductionRobustEstimation2012]
to limit the influence of extreme outliers without discarding
observations.

| Variable | Field(s) | Formula | Role |
|----------|----------|---------|------|
| Capital Intensity | `capx`, `at` | `capx / at` | Dependent (Y) |
| GPR Index | GPR | Annual average | Independent (X) |
| GPR × Size | — | `gpr × ln_at` | H2 interaction |
| Firm Size | `at` | `log(at)` | Moderator + Control |
| Leverage | `dltt`, `at` | `dltt / at` | Control |
| RoA | `nicon`, `at` | `nicon / at` | Control |
| Cash Ratio | `che`, `at` | `che / at` | Control |

: Variable definitions. Source: Compustat Global (WRDS); GPR index
from @caldaraMeasuringGeopoliticalRisk2022.

## Estimation Strategy

We estimate a firm fixed effects panel model:

$$\text{CAPX}_{it} = \beta_1 \text{GPR}_{t} +
\beta_2 (\text{GPR} \times \text{Size})_{it} +
\gamma X_{it} + \mu_i + \varepsilon_{it}$$

where $\mu_i$ are firm fixed effects absorbing time-invariant
heterogeneity. Year fixed effects cannot be included because the GPR
index varies only at the annual level — adding year dummies would
perfectly absorb the GPR variable [@mundlakPoolingTimeSeries1978].
Standard errors are clustered at the firm level
[@petersenEstimatingStandardErrors2009].

# Results

## Descriptive Statistics

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics. Continuous variables winsorized at 1st–99th percentiles."
summary[["count","mean","std","min","50%","max"]].rename(
    columns={"count":"N","50%":"Median"}).round(3)
```

The mean capital intensity is `{python} capx_mean`, with a median of
`{python} capx_median`. The GPR index averaged `{python} gpr_mean`
across the sample period, with substantial variation driven by
geopolitical episodes including Brexit, the COVID-19 pandemic, and
the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine
[@caldaraMeasuringGeopoliticalRisk2022].

![GPR index versus capital intensity (left panel, with bin means) and
median capital intensity by GPR level for small versus large firms
(right panel).](output/figures/main_relationship.png){width=90%}

## Regression Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-regression
#| tbl-cap: "Panel regression results. Dependent variable: Capital Intensity (capx/at). Standard errors clustered at firm level in Models (2)–(3). * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01."
coef_rows = [r for r in results.index if r not in ["Firm FE","Year FE","Clustered SE","N","R²"]]
stat_rows = ["Firm FE","Year FE","Clustered SE","N","R²"]
results.loc[coef_rows + stat_rows, ["(1) OLS","(2) FE","(3) FE+H2"]]
```

**H1 — GPR and capital investment.** The GPR coefficient in the firm
fixed effects model (Model 2) is $\hat{\beta}_1 =$ `{python} b_gpr`
(p < 0.01). The sign is positive, contrary to the negative direction
predicted by H1. **H1 is not supported.** Rather than reducing capital
investment, higher geopolitical risk is associated with a small but
significant increase in capital intensity. One possible interpretation
is that SMEs respond to geopolitical uncertainty by substituting
domestic capital investment for international expansion, consistent
with the Uppsala model [@johansonInternationalizationProcessFirm1977].

The OLS estimate exceeds the FE estimate by `{python} int(bias_pct)`%,
confirming substantial omitted variable bias in the pooled
cross-section [@mundlakPoolingTimeSeries1978;
@petersenEstimatingStandardErrors2009].

**H2 — Firm size as moderator.** The interaction term (Model 3) is
not statistically significant (p = 0.889). **H2 is not supported.**

# Discussion and Conclusion

## Discussion

The unexpected positive sign on GPR merits theoretical reflection.
The real options framework predicts investment deferral under
uncertainty [@bloomImpactUncertaintyShocks2009], but this assumes the
marginal investment project is internationally oriented. If SMEs
respond to geopolitical uncertainty by redirecting investment toward
domestic operations — substituting foreign market entry for domestic
capacity expansion — aggregate capital intensity could rise even as
international investment falls. This interpretation is consistent with
the Uppsala model [@johansonInternationalizationProcessFirm1977] and
with evidence that policy uncertainty redirects rather than simply
reduces investment [@gulenPolicyUncertaintyCorporate2015].

## Conclusion

This research note examines the effect of geopolitical uncertainty on
capital investment among `{python} f"{n_firms:,}"` European SMEs over
2015–2024. Contrary to H1, higher geopolitical risk is associated with
a small but significant increase in capital intensity within firms
($\hat{\beta} =$ `{python} b_gpr`, p < 0.01). H2 — that firm size
moderates this relationship — is not supported.

Two limitations apply. First, the GPR index is a global measure that
does not distinguish between geopolitical events affecting specific
European countries differently; country-specific GPR measures would
provide finer identification. Second, capital expenditure from
Compustat captures tangible investment only; intangible investment
responses to geopolitical uncertainty remain unobserved. Future
research could exploit sudden geopolitical shocks — such as the 2022
Russian invasion of Ukraine — as natural experiments to identify
causal effects.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
